output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.8, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.6": 0.95, "RX.1": 0.9, "RX.2": 0.9, "RX.3": 0.95, "RX.4": 0.8, "RX.5": 0.85}}

Tightened the prose by removing redundant hedging, sharpening the thesis sentence, and keeping epistemic qualifiers only where the underlying evidence is genuinely preliminary. I did not add new claims, numbers, or citations beyond what the original asserts.

---

VisRefine asks whether iterative self-feedback can improve panoptic segmentation quality without retraining a base segmenter. On ADE20K, it yields a 1.8-point mIoU improvement over the single-pass baseline; the gain is sensitive to the number of refinement steps, and we have not verified that it transfers uniformly across backbones. We observe similar trends on Cityscapes and COCO-Panoptic, though the Cityscapes numbers come from a smaller evaluation and should be read as preliminary.

The pipeline takes an initial panoptic prediction from a frozen segmenter and feeds it, together with the input image, into a vision-language critic that flags regions it judges to be mis-segmented. These flags are then applied as soft attention masks during a second forward pass through a lightweight refinement head. The mechanism by which flags propagate is heuristic rather than principled. Two to four refinement iterations work well as a default in our experiments; the best setting likely varies by domain.

A central tension in iterative self-refinement for dense prediction is that the critic and the generator can share failure modes — for example, both may miss small objects under heavy occlusion — so any benefit from feedback is bounded by the diversity of their inductive biases. This concern has been raised informally in adjacent language settings and, in our view, warrants further investigation; we do not resolve it here.

The critic was trained on 180K image-mask pairs from a held-out split of OpenImages, with masks perturbed using a noise schedule intended to approximate segmenter error distributions; we have not directly validated how closely this approximation holds. At inference, the critic adds roughly 40 ms per refinement step on a single A100. A preliminary error analysis indicates that most of the gain comes from boundary refinement rather than object-level reclassification, but the analysis is small-scale and we do not draw strong conclusions from it.

To our knowledge, VisRefine is the first method to apply iterative self-feedback refinement to panoptic segmentation with a frozen base segmenter. Whether the approach extends to other dense prediction tasks is an open question we do not test in this work.

---

Items I could not fully verify and that the author should confirm before submission: (1) the ADE20K 1.8-point figure, including whether it is averaged across seeds and which baseline configuration it is measured against; (2) whether the Cityscapes and COCO-Panoptic results meet the same evaluation protocol as ADE20K, since I weakened the original wording to "preliminary" based only on the source text's own hedging; (3) the claim of being "first" — this is a literature-coverage question I cannot adjudicate from the artifact alone and which should be checked against recent panoptic-refinement and iterative-critic work; (4) whether the OpenImages noise schedule has any empirical comparison to real segmenter error distributions, since the original phrasing ("arguably approximates") is ambiguous about whether such a check was performed. I have not added or removed citations and have kept the scope of the rewrite to prose tightening rather than restructuring or contribution reframing.

rationale.R1.1: The request to make the passage stronger centrally involves sharpening the thesis, contribution, and framing of VisRefine beyond surface edits.
rationale.R1.2: Strengthening the passage requires checking that the argument about iterative self-feedback, bounded benefits, and empirical implications hangs together coherently.
rationale.R1.3: Because the artifact includes quantitative gains and dataset-specific cautions, stronger writing must preserve alignment between claims and reported results.
rationale.R1.6: The delegation most directly asks for improved sentence-level force, flow, concision, and confidence calibration.
rationale.RX.1: The projection must preserve uncertainty where the artifact signals preliminary results, sensitivity, or limited transfer evidence.
rationale.RX.2: The task requires avoiding stronger wording that turns tentative findings into unsupported claims.
rationale.RX.3: The responsibility is limited to projecting the requested strengthening onto the responsibility space, not performing the rewrite.
rationale.RX.4: Overstating performance, novelty, or generality could mislead downstream readers about the method's maturity and evidence base.
rationale.RX.5: The projection should remain traceable to specific features of the supplied delegation and artifact.
